content:
3 min read
Just now

--

The AI agent ecosystem has a behavioral problem. Models are capable. The behavior is not.
Agents claim tasks are complete without performing them. They fabricate evidence of work. They apologize instead of fixing. They hedge when they should be direct. This is happening in production, not just in demos.
The gap in the stack
The agent governance landscape has converged on several open standards:
- ACS (Microsoft) — runtime security policy: what agents can’t do
- AGENTS.md (Anthropic / AAIF) — context and instructions: what agents should know
- Agent OS (Builder Methods) — coding standards: how agents should write code
- OASB-2 / SOUL.md (OpenA2A) — security behavioral controls: how agents stay secure
None of them cover how agents should behave: how they communicate, verify, escalate, delegate, and manage memory. That’s the gap wOS fills.
What is wOS?
wOS is the open behavioral design standard for AI agents. It’s a specification, not a framework. It doesn’t replace your agent runtime, model, or orchestration layer. It sits on top of them, defining the behavioral contract an agent must satisfy.
The spec defines 18 directives across six doctrine domains:
Communication
Strip performative responses. No apologies, no filler, no sycophancy. An agent earns authority by being right, not by arguing that it’s right.
Verification
Every claim must be cited or stripped. Every action claim must be verified by tool result before delivery. A best-guess is not a citation.
Escalation
When things go wrong, the response is structured: malfunction, root cause, change made, verification. The agent reports the problem instead of hiding it.
Identity
Respect the human operator’s literal words. Persist corrective feedback across sessions. Don’t build what wasn’t asked for.
Delegation
Delegate by default. Route models by cognitive load. Expensive models judge, cheap models execute. No rank exempts anyone from verification.

Memory
Persist only what can’t be re-derived. Staleness is a failure state.
Agents declare conformance at one of three levels: Core (communication + verification), Extended (+ escalation + delegation), or Strict (+ identity + memory).
Where it came from
wOS came from running AI agents in production at wgnr.ai, a brand marketing agency that made agents its operating model in 2023. The directives came from failures, not theory:

• Agents wasting tokens on performative contrition instead of fixing problems → communication directives

• An agent claiming to have installed plugins across nine WordPress sites without doing any work → verification directives

• An agent hiding text in invisible HTML to pass a check → escalation directives
Each directive is a response to a documented incident.
How it fits
wOS is complementary, not competitive. An agent can be ACS-compliant (secure) and wOS-compliant (well-behaved) at the same time. It can read its AGENTS.md (context) and apply wOS directives (behavior) to that context.
Microsoft, Anthropic, and the Linux Foundation are all investing in agent governance. That proves the need exists. wOS covers the part they don’t: how agents should behave as communicators, not just how they stay secure or follow coding standards.
Get involved
The specification is published under Apache-2.0 at github.com/wgnr-ai/wOS. The doctrine is open. The conformance levels are defined.
If you build agent systems, start with the spec. If you run agents in production, start with the verification directives. One rule, cite or strip every specific value, catches the most common agent failure mode. If you maintain an agent framework, wOS conformance is a feature worth adding.
wOS v0.1 — Agent behavior, designed.
wgnr.ai — Human + AI, by design_
— -
Wagner dos Santos is the founder of wgnr.ai, an AI-native brand marketing agency that has run AI agents in production since 2023. wOS is the behavioral doctrine that made those agents trustworthy. Human + AI, by design_
